answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation*, Vol. II (1826), and Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie sociale*, Vol. I (1825), offer complementary perspectives on human development, though neither presents a strict "stadial history" in the Scottish Enlightenment mold (e.g., hunting → herding → farming → commerce).**[^1][^2]

Comte's Vol. II is an ethnographic survey of global "races" (varieties/species: copper-colored/American, Malay/Oceanian, black/African), organized by latitude, climate, and **means of subsistence** (fishing/hunting vs. rudimentary agriculture). He rejects simplistic climate determinism (contra Montesquieu), attributing moral, intellectual, and social "perfection/degradation" primarily to physical constitution, local circumstances, and subsistence mode.[^3] Societies progress from scarcity-driven anarchy (hunters/fishers: no fixed authority, rule by personal strength, endemic vengeance/perfidy) to rudimentary hierarchy/property under agriculture.[^4]

Dunoyer, explicitly citing Comte's *Traité*, builds a **normative theory of liberty** across societal "ways of life" (*maniè̀re de vivre*): savage hunters (least free: voracity, infanticide, extermination wars); nomads/herders (slight progress: enslavement over massacre); sedentary slaveholders/serfs (more security, but oppression); and "purely industrious" peoples (maximal liberty: forces directed to things, not persons; associations for production, not plunder).[^5] Liberty grows as faculties develop, subsistence stabilizes, and violence wanes.

### Reading Alongside: Complementarity and Structure
- **Comte as Empirical Foundation**: Vol. II supplies Dunoyer's anthropological data. Comte's chapters 13–27 map "moral development" (*développement moral*) and governments by subsistence/race (e.g., North American copper-colored fishers/hunters: familial patriarchy only, no tribal authority; equatorial agriculturists: private property, hierarchies).[^6] This prefigures Dunoyer's stages, grounding them in traveler accounts (Hearne, Cook, Péron) rather than conjecture.
- **Dunoyer as Theoretical Synthesis**: Dunoyer's Vol. I (chs. 5–12) abstracts Comte's ethnography into a liberty metric: freedom ∝ faculty development + non-harmful use. He critiques Rousseau/Montesquieu (echoing Comte) for romanticizing savagery/nomadism.[^7] Comte describes *is*; Dunoyer prescribes *ought* (industry maximizes liberty).

Both reject innate "state of nature" liberty (Rousseau): early stages are violent/miserable due to scarcity.

### Typological Convergences
Their schemas align on **subsistence-driven progression** from anarchy to order/liberty:

| Stage/Subsistence | Comte (Vol. II: e.g., Chs. 14–18, Copper/Malay Races) | Dunoyer (Vol. I: Explicit Stages) | Shared Traits/Liberty Level |
|--------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Hunter/Fisher (Scarcity: Game/Fish Primary)** | Northern copper-colored (Ch. 14): No tribal authority; familial patriarchy ends at independence; disputes by vengeance/assassination; perfidy/cannibalism in famine; women/children as property seized by strength.[^8] Moral degradation from insecurity. | Savage (Ch. 5): Least free; voracity, abandonment/infanticide, extermination wars; women beasts-of-burden; no calculation.[^9] | **Low liberty**: Anarchy (strength rules); violence endemic; faculties stunted by famine/idleness. Progress via agriculture. |
| **Pastoral/Nomadic (Herds + Rudimentary Hunt)** | Implicit (e.g., Malay relations, Chs. 20–22): More calculation (enslavement > massacre); hereditary chiefs (weak authority); still migratory/warlike.[^10] | Nomad (Ch. 6): More free than savages; herds enable enslavement (better than death); less infanticide; but brigandage/war constant.[^11] | **Moderate gain**: Security via mobility/slaves; violence tempers (interest > fury); faculties slightly extended. |
| **Agricultural/Sedentary (Crops + Hunt/Fish)** | Equatorial copper/Malay (Chs. 18–19, 25): Private land; classes (aristocrats/slaves); federations; vices from abundance/sloth.[^12] | Slaveholder/Serf (Chs. 7–10): Compatible with liberty (masters free via slaves); property/serfdom stabilizes; obstacles remain.[^13] | **Rising liberty**: Property/hierarchy; fewer scarcities; moral progress (less extermination). |
| **Advanced/Industrial (Full Production)** | Implicit (civilized previews, Chs. 1–2, 12): Intellectual/moral perfection; governments secure property.[^14] | Purely Industrious (Ch. 11): Maximal liberty; forces on *things*; associations productive; government minimal (represses violence only).[^15] | **High liberty**: Faculties fully developed; peace via exchange; no oppression. |

**Core Convergence**: Subsistence dictates social form/morals/liberty. Hunters: "no authority... inequality of strength" (Comte); "less free" (Dunoyer). Agriculture/industry: property, security, faculty growth. Both see universal progression potential, modulated by race/climate (Comte emphasizes races; Dunoyer race + circumstance). Divergence: Comte descriptive/ethnographic; Dunoyer prescriptive (industry as telos).

The corpus lacks Comte's other volumes or Dunoyer's Vol. II for fuller synthesis, but these align as industrialist anthropology vs. liberty theory.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [ch.1](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch.5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/05-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-pe/translation/).

[^3]: Ibid., [ch.13](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/13-du-developpement-moral-des-peuples-de-diverses-esp/translation/#p-d52288).

[^4]: Ibid., [ch.14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/14-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-fc5016).

[^5]: Dunoyer, [ch.1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-341c27); cites Comte.

[^6]: Comte, [ch.13–14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/13-du-developpement-moral-des-peuples-de-diverses-esp/translation/).

[^7]: Dunoyer, [ch.5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/05-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-pe/translation/#p-38f9f0).

[^8]: Comte, [ch.14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/14-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-fc5016).

[^9]: Dunoyer, [ch.5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/05-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-pe/translation/#p-3a6ae9).

[^10]: Comte, [ch.20](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/20-des-relations-qui-existent-entre-les-deux-sexes-ch/translation/).

[^11]: Dunoyer, [ch.6](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/06-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-a96ef2).

[^12]: Comte, [ch.18](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/18-de-l-etat-social-et-des-moeurs-des-peuples-d-espec/translation/).

[^13]: Dunoyer, [ch.8](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/08-iii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-de/translation/).

[^14]: Comte, [ch.1](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/#p-178259).

[^15]: Dunoyer, [ch.11](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-40e4c5).
